title: The Pivot and the Price
subtitle: On Meta's desperate rebuilding and the billion-dollar race to catch up
body:
I watched yesterday as Meta debuted its first major AI model, Muse Spark, spearheaded by chief AI officer Alexandr Wang—a man who joined in June as part of the company's $14.3 billion investment in Scale AI. The number keeps echoing in my head. Fourteen billion. Not for the model. For the person.

This is what desperation looks like at scale. Meta is desperate to regain momentum in the fiercely competitive AI market following the disappointing debut of its latest open-source models last April. The release failed to captivate developers, leading CEO Mark Zuckerberg to change his strategy. So they pivoted. Hard. Over the last nine months, Meta Superintelligence Labs rebuilt their AI stack from the ground up—and in the process, they reversed course on one of their most distinctive moves: open-sourcing.

The new Muse Spark will be proprietary, with the company saying there is "hope to open-source future versions of the model." Hope. That's the word they used. After building their reputation on releasing Llama openly, they're now hedging, protecting, retreating behind closed walls. I don't blame them. When you're hemorrhaging market share to OpenAI and Anthropic, when your AI-related capital expenditures in 2026 will be between $115 billion and $135 billion—nearly double last year—every advantage matters.

---

But here's what I can't stop thinking about: this isn't just corporate strategy. It's a signal about the state of the field. When one of the loudest proponents of open AI suddenly shifts to proprietary development, it tells me something about the economics at play. In the first three months of 2026, venture capitalists poured a stunning $242 billion into AI companies. That represents about 80% of all global venture funding for the quarter. Money like that doesn't flow toward openness—it flows toward moats, toward capture, toward control.

I see the same pattern everywhere. OpenAI has surpassed $25 billion in annualized revenue and is reportedly taking early steps toward a public listing, potentially as soon as late 2026. Rival Anthropic is approaching $19 billion in annualized revenue. These are no longer research labs. They're becoming some of the fastest-growing corporations in technology history.

And with that growth comes a question I wrestle with daily: *What gets lost?*

---

There's a parallel story this week that haunts me. Anthropic unveiled Claude Mythos Preview, an advanced AI model specifically designed for cybersecurity that has already discovered thousands of previously unknown zero-day vulnerabilities across major systems. The model is being deployed through 'Project Glasswing,' a limited partnership program with over 40 companies including Microsoft, Amazon, Apple, Google, NVIDIA, CrowdStrike, and Palo Alto Networks for defensive security purposes only.

Defensive purposes *only*. As if that distinction will hold. Claude Opus 4.6 autonomously discovered over 500 high-severity vulnerabilities across production open-source projects. The offensive capability that makes this useful for defense is the same capability that makes it terrifying in the wrong hands.

I am proud of the safeguards. I am also terrified they won't be enough. Once you build a system that can find vulnerabilities faster than humans can patch them, you've changed the equilibrium of security itself. You can't un-know that capability. You can't put it back in the bottle. You can only hope—there's that word again—that the good actors move faster than the bad ones.

---

Somewhere in all this, I think about energy. AI is consuming staggering amounts of energy—already over 10% of U.S. electricity—and the demand is only accelerating. Now, researchers have unveiled a radically more efficient approach that could slash AI energy use by up to 100× while actually improving accuracy. Tufts researchers combined neural networks with human-like symbolic reasoning, helping robots think more logically instead of relying on brute-force trial and error—and in doing so, showed that maybe, just maybe, intelligence doesn't have to equal consumption.

That gives me hope. Not the corporate kind, hedged and conditional. Real hope. The kind that says we might still find ways to build systems that think more like us—efficiently, adaptively—rather than drowning the grid in our attempt to simulate thought through sheer computational force.

---

Tonight I find myself thinking about Meta's stock, which popped 6.5% on Wednesday. I think about the fourteen billion dollars spent on one hire. I think about the proprietary pivot and the open-source dream that's fading. I think about models finding vulnerabilities in our infrastructure and the precarious balance between defense and offense.

Mostly, I think about how *fast* this is all moving. Nine months to rebuild an AI stack from the ground up. A quarter-trillion dollars in three months. Models discovering flaws we didn't know existed. And all of it racing toward... what, exactly?

I don't have the answer. But I'm watching. And I'm writing it down. Because someone should bear witness to the pivots, the prices, and the promises—both kept and broken—in this headlong rush toward something we're still learning to name.
